Forward Surgical Saturation under Large-Scale Combat Operations

A doctrine-grounded abandonment-queue study of the casualty-treatment cliff.
Austin Semmel
Origin: KSIL Q187 (The Army Strategy, High-Intensity Conflict) + SSE26 Q522 (Office of the Surgeon General G-33) · Target: Military Operations Research
Overview
Explore
Analysis
Back matter

1 · Framing

A great-power war could deliver wounded faster than a forward surgical team can operate. Whether it does, and by how much, is what we test. We are left to determine which casualties died of preventable wounds, the ones faster or better-arranged care would have saved, and which of non-preventable ones. Force design can reduce only the preventable deaths, which a capacity count misses. This study estimates how many there are, and which interventions prevent the most: faster evacuation, more surgery, prolonged field care, or how the teams are positioned.

Three findings drive the resourcing decision:

  • Size for the surge. A team that keeps pace with the daily average can be overwhelmed by one large arrival of casualties at once. Averaging over an entire day can mask the risk from those arrival surges.
  • Speed beats capacity. Time from wounding to the table matters more for survival than the number of tables. In our model the first hour of delay costs about 16 mortality points, more than a second table would save.
  • Evacuation has two halves with opposite fixes. Moving the wounded to surgery is threat-limited: contested air keeps the helicopters on the ground. Moving them out of theater is lift-limited: the rear hospitals have capacity to spare, but long-range aircraft are few. A fix for one does nothing for the other.

What the sponsors asked, verbatim

"Assess the strategic impact of high casualty rates sustained in a short period against a great power, where contested domains or burgeoning logistical requirements prevent the rapid evacuation of the wounded."

Q187, Key Strategic Issues List 2021–2022, p.29 · sponsor: The Army Strategy, D. High-Intensity Conflict

"Medical Operations in LSCO. How does the US Army Medical Corps need to adapt to LSCO? What structural or organizational adjustments are necessary to account for the expected casualties of modern war?"

Q522, AY2025–26 Annual Estimate (SSE), p.98 · sponsor: Office of the Surgeon General, G-33

2 · The decision this serves

The question that settles the resourcing call
At what casualty arrival rate does a forward surgical team flip from wound-limited death, set by physiology, to capacity-limited death, set by waiting? And which lever moves that flip point the most?

The flip separates the deaths into two groups. Below it, wound biology sets the toll, and more resources save almost no one. Above it, the team's shortfall adds preventable deaths, the share that force structure, positioning, evacuation policy, and triage can reduce. Summed over a campaign, it is combat power lost for nothing: Q187's "strategic impact," Q522's "structural adjustment." Today's teams are sized near the peer planning casualty rate, while high-intensity offensive operations run several times higher, so the flip is already in range within the engagement pulse, even where the daily average still has margin.

Who decides, and what they decide

Decision-makerThe choice this study informs
Army force designersHow many forward surgical teams to build and field.
Office of the Surgeon General, G-33How far forward to position surgery and how widely to disperse it.
Theater surgeon / SecDefWhat evacuation policy to set when contested transport lengthens holding time.

Why now: the LSCO pivot away from two decades of air supremacy and golden-hour evacuation. Ukraine and Gaza supply the two boundary cases, and Q187 and Q522 are live sponsor asks.

3 · The conditions that decide the answer

The casualty-rate regime is the condition that turns surgical capacity from surplus to binding. At the peer planning rate the team has margin; at high-intensity offensive rates the engaged-echelon pulse overruns it. The analysis sweeps the rate rather than betting on one scenario. Contested forward air is the second condition: it lengthens the time the wounded spend reaching surgery, which is where most of the preventable death comes from.

"Air" means two different aircraft, and the distinction runs through the whole analysis.

  • Forward, getting the wounded to surgery (point of injury → team): threat-limited. The lift is rotary-wing casualty evacuation; contested air over the close fight grounds or downs it, and the wounded die in transit. These fixes need no contested route: prolonged field care, blood pushed forward, dispersion.
  • Rear, getting the wounded out (team → definitive care): capacity-limited. The lift is fixed-wing strategic transport flying from rear airfields; the rear hospitals flex, and a finite airlift fleet is what binds. The fix is procurement: airframes and the en-route care crews who ride them.

So "air superiority" in this study is the narrow thing the forward axis needs, a permissive environment for rotary casualty evacuation over the close fight. These are the two halves of Q187's "contested domains or burgeoning logistical requirements," and they take opposite fixes.

4 · The levers

Each lever acts on one piece of the problem. The decisive question for force design is whether a lever moves the flip point itself or only the death level above it.

LeverOwnerWhat it moves
Surgical capacity (teams per force)Force designPushes the flip point to a higher casualty rate.
Forward positioning & dispersionForce designLowers arrivals per team and shortens the leg to surgery; trades against distance to care.
Evacuation policy (days held)SecDef / theaterSets holding time; can shift the binding constraint to the rear pipeline.
Strategic evacuation airliftProcurementClears theater holding and relieves back-pressure on the forward tables (the rear, capacity-limited axis).
Forward casualty evacuation (POI→team)Contested by the enemySets transit time and prehospital death (the forward, threat-limited axis); cannot be bought with aircraft the enemy downs first.
Triage disciplineDoctrineLowers the death level above the flip point.
Resupply (blood, Class VIII)SustainmentHolds surgical throughput up over time, keeping the flip point from drifting toward lower rates.

The mechanism behind every row is the same: the team is a queue in which the wounded can die waiting, an abandonment queue. The Methods tab formalizes it; the Sim tab lets you turn the dials; the Posture tab shows the consolidate-versus-distribute crossover.

Mₓ / G / 2 / ∞  +  G
1The switches below build that string. A/B/c/K + abandonment: arrival process / service distribution / number of tables / waiting-room size, then the survival clock. The node is a Forward Resuscitative Surgical Detachment: 2 tables, ~135 min/case (ATP 4-02.25; Hall 2023). It defaults to the thesis configuration: casualties arrive in batches (mass-casualty pulses), ~30% need an OR table, and the untreated die when their survival clock runs out.

The switches

Arrivals : slot A
load λ
2casualties/day reaching the node. Steady = one at a time (Poisson); Batch = one event, many wounded, same daily average, pulsed.
batch median
tail σ
max event
3Bus size is a discretized lognormal, ≥1 (no empty bus), truncated at max event: no single event puts more than that many wounded on one node at once. σ is the tail: low = even trickle, high = rare mass-casualty pulses at the same average.
Surgical funnel : fₛₐₕₘ
operative fraction
2Share of presenting casualties needing an OR table, measured ~0.30 (Israel/Ukraine). The rest are triaged or return to duty and never queue. Node load = λ × fₛₐₕₘ.
Casualty evacuation : two tiers
air-evac fraction
air lift
ground lift
2This dial is air superiority. The fraction lifted within the golden hour by air; the rest go by ground, which is slower. The clock runs from wounding, so transit consumes survival time: a casualty whose clock reaches zero in transit dies before reaching the node (prehospital). Afghanistan ≈ 0.9+; contested LSCO ≈ 0.2.
Tables : slot c
servers c
case time 1/μ
2Table-limited capacity λ* = cμ. Push 1/μ toward 180 for the LSCO severity shift + contested-rear de-rate.
Strategic evacuation : out of theater
strategic AE lift
post-op beds
time-to-flight
5Drives the full-chain view (dimmed when zoomed into the single node). The rear bottleneck is the Air Force fixed-wing litter fleet (✈). Hospital beds are not the binding constraint; airframes are, and no one targets them, there simply aren't enough. Cut strategic AE lift and the queue backs up the chain: stabilized patients can't fly out, theater holding fills, post-op beds fill, and a recovered Role 2 patient cannot leave the table because no bed is open to receive him. The table is occupied though no surgery is underway, and a forward casualty dies waiting for it. More lift saves more lives than more cots.
Service shape : slot B
variability CV
3M is a solvable birth-death chain and G is not, but the overload answer barely changes. Lognormal (Hall case-mix) is the grounded default.
Survival clock : from wounding
survival span
4How long the wounded survive untreated, timed from the moment of wounding, the golden hour for hemorrhage. Transit consumes part of this time; what remains is the clock left at the node. Reach zero → died-of-wounds. The hazard rises with elapsed time, so the memoryless exponential understates late deaths and +G is the default.
Waiting room : slot K
capacity K
1Cap the room and overflow is turned away, a different loss than dying in line.
Random seed
seed
Batch, service, and patience draws are seeded, so the same seed repeats a run. Speed lives under the animation.

The full chain, live

FRONT LINEpoint of wounding
ROLE 1aid · TCCC
EVACUATIONair ▲ / ground ▼
ROLE 2forward surgery · you're inside ▼
ROLE 3definitive · rear
The panel below is the inside of that lit Role 2 box: arrivals, the waiting bay, the operating tables. Switch to Full chain to see this node embedded in the echelons.
Casualties arrive at left in pulses. Grey ones are triaged and pass through (non-operative). The ~30% needing surgery wait in the bay (each with a draining survival bar) and reach a table when one frees. A bar that empties turns red: that casualty died of wounds. Served casualties leave right, green.
FRONT LINEpoint of wounding
ROLE 1aid · TCCC
EVACUATIONair ▲ / ground ▼
ROLE 2forward surgery
ROLE 3definitive · rear
air-evac casualty ground-evac casualty triaged / RTD in surgery stabilized → rear died of wounds ▔ survival clock (drains in transit & in line)
0
Surgical arrivals
0/2
On tables
0
Waiting
0
Survived
0
Died of wounds
0
Turned away
–
Speed 60 min/s sim-clock: 0 h

What the math says · exact, from a 30,000-case run at these settings

–
load ρ = λfₛₐₕₘ/cμ
–
capacity λ* = cμ /day
–
table utilization
–
P(wait) find no table
–
mean wait (served)
–
mean line length Lq
–
node DOW | reached
–
overload law (1−cμ/λ)⁺
3This pulse stream: mean bus – casualties · – chance one bus alone overflows both tables (batch ≥ 2c) · – buses/day.
4Where the dying happens: – die in transit (prehospital, never reach a table) · – die waiting at the node · – total of operative casualties. Lower the air-evac fraction and the deaths move from the node into transit, because slower lifts kill before arrival.
4λ*=cμ is the table-limited (surge) ceiling: ≈21/day at 2 tables. Doctrine's sustained figure is ~10/day; beds, blood, and the 72-h rest cycle bind first. Turn the survival clock to None and push ρ past 1: Lq runs away. Abandonment is what makes an overloaded war finite: the death fraction is the answer.

Policy lab · pin a baseline, then play; the model grades the marginal value

5Set your conditions (air superiority, load, batch) and pin them as the baseline. Then apply an intervention and read its marginal value: the change in mortality against that fixed baseline. Each policy is one model lever; the engine scores it, it does not choose for you.
No baseline pinned yet.
–
Each button is the baseline plus one change: PFC doubles the survival span; whole blood lengthens it ~40% and shortens cases; faster ground evac halves the ground lift; more tables adds two; damage-control cuts case time by a third. Move any slider yourself for free play, and the marginal value still reads against the pinned baseline. (Same seed for both, so the Δ is a clean paired comparison.)

The frontier · the policy ranking reverses as air superiority falls

5Each line is one policy's marginal value (mortality points saved versus doing nothing) swept across the air-evac fraction, your pinned baseline otherwise fixed. The curves cross: node-side levers (more tables, damage-control) save the most when the air-evac fraction is high and fall toward zero as it drops; transit and clock levers (prolonged field care, faster ground, whole blood) gain as it drops, because more of the dying then happens before the node. At the crossing the best policy changes. What to buy depends on whether you will hold the air.

The elbow

DOW at your σσ=0.6 (light tail)σ=1.4 (heavy tail)fluid law (1−1/ρ)⁺
5Below ρ=1 the daily average keeps up, yet a heavy batch tail still kills, because a line forms inside each pulse even when the average has slack. Overlay the σ sweep: the heavier the tail, the higher the death curve at the same ρ. The dashed fluid law is the many-server limit; with only two tables the simulated elbow is rounded and sits above it.
One node, FIFO within the waiting bay; the paper's model adds severity priority (a clearing rule over impatient jobs), the network of echelons, and a severity-indexed survival clock. The numbers panel runs the exact discrete-event engine; the animation is the same logic slowed down to watch. Batch sizes are a discretized lognormal (median and tail σ set above); service and patience draws are seeded, so a given seed repeats.

Posture: consolidate or distribute?

The Army holds a fixed amount of forward surgery. It can consolidate that capacity into a few large hospitals or distribute it into many small forward teams. Consolidation pools servers: a larger team idles less between cases and works off a surge faster. Distribution shortens the trip: the wounded travel less distance to a table. Which one saves more lives depends on whether the Army holds air superiority.

Air superiority decides which posture wins. Drag the control to watch the curves cross; each point is the model's validated rate per 1,000 reaching surgery (Table D-1). Switch loads to see the crossover hold from Korea to Italy.

Consolidated · 1 hospital
–
Distributed · 8 teams
–
Better posture
–
Faint gray lines are the in-between postures (2 and 4 nodes); more nodes trade pooling efficiency for proximity. Crossover is where the consolidated and distributed curves meet.

2 · Plan

The question, the hypotheses, and the limits were all fixed before the model ran.

The research question

The research question
In a great-power war, does the casualty rate outrun the Army's forward surgical capacity, and at what point does it break?
Made precise: find the casualty arrival rate $\lambda^{*}$ at which forward surgical capacity saturates, and the rate at which the wounded die-of-wounds once it does, both as a function of how long contested transport holds casualties before evacuation.

The answer is three numbers:

  • The threshold $\lambda^{*}$ at which operating-table-hours saturate (utilization $\rho\to 1$) and the died-of-wounds fraction transitions sharply.
  • The curve $\text{DOW}(\lambda,T)$ above that threshold: how many more die per increment of overload.
  • The evacuation-policy sensitivity $\partial\,\text{DOW}/\partial T$: added deaths per added holding-day.

Scope is the forward surgical node in a single theater, concretely the Forward Resuscitative Surgical Detachment (FRSD), the surgical team that augments a Role 2 Enhanced medical company, whose published signature (2 tables, 135 min/case, ~10 cases/24 h) sets the capacity throughout. The roles-of-care tandem (FRSD → post-surgical holding → onward) enters as an extension. Downstream, strategic aeromedical-evacuation lift out of theater binds before beds do, because definitive-care beds flex and offload to civilian systems while the airlift fleet does not. Sizing that fleet is an adjacent problem we leave to other work.

How the question answers each sponsor ask

Each clause of the two sponsor questions maps onto a parameter or a hypothesis.

The sponsor's operative clauseHow the model answers it, specifically
Q187: "high casualty rates sustained in a short period"The arrival stream $\lambda(t)$. Lower-intensity offensive rates from ATP 4-02.55 (Table D-4h, Korea, WIA ≈ 3.54/1,000/day)ATP 4-02.55 and Jones's echelon ratesJones'19 set $\lambda$; "short period" is the time-varying burst ($M_t$). The lead test is the inequality $\lambda_{\text{obs}}>\lambda^{*}$.
Q187: "contested domains or burgeoning logistical requirements … prevent the rapid evacuation of the wounded"Two failure modes, taken separately. Contested domains degrade the forward leg, point of injury to the node: the threat-limited air-superiority axis, where the wounded die in transit. Burgeoning logistical requirements saturate the rearward leg, out of theater: the capacity-limited strategic-airlift axis, where stabilized patients back up for want of airframes. Both lengthen time-in-system $T$ (FM 4-02 §8-5)FM 4-02 and turn waiting into death; the model returns $\partial\,\text{DOW}/\partial T$ and the air-evac sensitivity (H4).
Q187: "Assess the strategic impact"The estimand made numeric: $\lambda^{*}$, the casualty rate at which the golden-hour regime collapses, and the curve $\text{DOW}(\lambda,T)$. The impact is a rate and a death fraction.
Q522: "What structural or organizational adjustments are necessary"The binding-constraint result (H3): operating-table-hours saturate before beds, so the adjustment is surgical throughput, teams and tables, the very thing the force does not report. $\lambda^{*}=s\mu$ sets how much.
Q522: "to account for the expected casualties of modern war"$\lambda$ is calibrated to observed LSCO and peer data, so the capacity target $s\mu \ge \lambda_{\text{obs}}$ is explicit and checkable.
Q232 (sibling): "high casualty producing munitions … MASCAL … in an A2AD environment"The batch/surge variant: large arrival pulses under the same contested-rear lever. Addressed as an extension; the sandbox's burst mode previews it.

Q522 is broad. The model answers its resourcing core, what to grow and by how much, and frames the wider organizational design.

Pre-registered hypotheses

Ordered so the lead claim is checkable against data that exists.

H1: saturation is a within-pulse phenomenon (the checkable lead). On daily-average load a single doctrinal node keeps margin even at high-intensity offensive rates; it saturates inside the engagement pulse, the great-power regime Q187 names, where casualties arrive far faster than tables turn over: $$\lambda_{\text{peak}} \;\gg\; s\mu \quad\text{inside the battle pulse.}$$ The calibration audit ran this check. The offensive WIA rate runs roughly 3–4× the lower-intensity rate (9.74–15.6 vs 3.54/1,000/day), but at a defensible surgical fraction the daily-average load stays under capacity: $\rho\approx 0.2$ at the Korea rate and $\rho\approx 0.4$–$0.8$ at WWII-offensive rates ($f_{\text{surg}}=0.10$–$0.12$, $s\mu=10$/day). The burst is what saturates the node while the daily-average load still has room: a measured pulse (Soroka 83 casualties/hr) exceeds node throughput (~0.4 surgical cases/hr) by one-to-two orders of magnitude, sharpened by $\mu$-degradation under a contested forward fight (see Results). Falsified if the node absorbs the engaged-echelon pulse without died-of-wounds rising.
H2: the cliff. Past the threshold, died-of-wounds rises sharply, an elbow in the curve: $$\text{DOW}(\lambda)\;\approx\;\left(1-\frac{s\mu}{\lambda}\right)^{\!+}.$$ Computed (Results): the threshold location holds at $\lambda^{*}=s\mu$ and the fluid law is the above-threshold asymptote, but the survival clock softens the kink, since abandonment is nonzero below $\rho=1$ (casualties renege while waiting). The elbow holds its location and bends more gently than the idealized law. Falsified if no transition appears; one does.
H3: the binding constraint. At the threshold, what binds is operating-table-hours. Falsified if beds saturate first under doctrinal ratios.
H4: the evacuation-timeline lever. Lengthening the time $T$ from wounding to surgery raises DOW at fixed $\lambda$: $$\frac{\partial\,\text{DOW}}{\partial T} \;>\; 0.$$ Computed and validated (Results): $\partial\,\text{DOW}/\partial T$ is steep and positive, ~16 mortality points per added hour in the first hour, flattening as it saturates, so the golden hour falls out of the model. The Gaza (fast) / Ukraine (slow) bracket reproduces the direction. Disentanglement: the pre-registered $T$ is the forward timeline (time-to-surgery), which the bracket tests; this hypothesis as first written also gestured at the doctrinal rear "days held" (ATP 4-02.55 Table D-1's rising "died-in-hospital" factor), but that rear effect is the emergent back-pressure of Results Finding 4, reported as structure.

The two to defend hardest in review: H1's checkable inequality and H3's binding-constraint flip.

The go/no-go test

Everything downstream depends on one inequality, computable from data in hand:
$$\lambda_{\text{obs}} \;\overset{?}{>}\; s\mu.$$
Compute $s\mu$ for a doctrinal node from ATP 4-02.25, compare to echelon casualty rates against published surgical throughput. The inequality clears, and the full estimand follows from the model: $\lambda^{*}=s\mu$, the DOW elbow, and the evacuation-timeline slope $\partial\,\text{DOW}/\partial T$ are computed in Results.

Constraints, limitations, assumptions

Assumptions behind the sharp tipping point

If X, Y, Z hold, then the node has a saturation tipping point at $\lambda^{*}=s\mu$: below it DOW is wound-limited and nearly flat in $\lambda$; above it DOW rises as $\approx(1-s\mu/\lambda)^{+}$.

  • X: tables bind before beds. The flow constraint (operating-table-hours) saturates before the stock constraint (holding beds). If beds bind first, the threshold and the governing lever change.
  • Y: above-threshold DOW is waiting-dominated. Once overloaded, the marginal death is a reneging death (ran out of survival window in the queue).
  • Z: work-conserving triage; finite-mean service and survival. No table sits idle while a casualty waits, and $1/\mu$ and $\theta$ have finite means. This makes the overload limit clean and distribution-free to first order.

The modeling choices, each varied in a sensitivity check rather than fit to the data:

  • Queue discipline: the baseline engine serves first-in-first-out within the waiting bay; severity-priority table precedence and air-slot triage are implemented in a committed precedence engine (triage_test.js), and the precedence result (modest, rule-dependent, with naive "sickest-first" air routing backfiring) is reported in Results.
  • Arrival process: nonstationary / batch to match the observed pulses, with plain-Poisson as a robustness comparison.
  • Service model: $M/M/c+M$ (Erlang-A) for the closed-form threshold; $M/G/c+G$ (Whitt fluid) for the distribution-general check.
  • One representative node, stationary within a battle pulse; the network is future work.

Constraints: the hard bounds that fence the levers

  • Wound biology: the survival window $\theta(\text{severity})$ is physiology (hemorrhage timelines, the golden hour). We race it.
  • Minimum service time $1/\mu$: damage-control surgery takes what it takes (Hall: ~94–206 min by ISS)Hall'23. Compressible only so far.
  • Surgical-team inventory: the total pool of teams caps the capacity-allocation lever.
  • Lift / transport: forward casualty evacuation under contested air, and strategic airlift out of theater, caps evacuation throughput and so caps how short the evacuation policy can be.
  • Three endurance limits, kept distinct: (a) the patient-holding window, the FRSD/Role 2 holds a casualty up to 72 h before onward evacuation (ATP 4-02.5); (b) the equipment-set endurance, ≈30 cases / 72 h before the detachment must rest and resupply (ATP 4-02.25)ATP 4-02.25; (c) the Role 1 / casualty-collection-point hold that prolonged field care imposes forward when evacuation fails. The first two bound sustained $\mu$; the third is the forward-axis lever (see Results).

Limitations: what the study cannot settle

The DOW magnitude is unvalidated. No one publishes contested-rear clinical DOW rates, and Ukraine's outcome denominators are closed. The DOW magnitude is therefore a derived output with no real-world figure to check it against. The threshold $\lambda^{*}=s\mu$ is capacity against arrivals; it does not depend on the unobserved survival rate at all. That rate sets only the steepness of the curve above the threshold, so the saturation point holds whatever its true value. Because the magnitude cannot be validated directly, we lead with the checkable inequality $\lambda_{\text{obs}}>s\mu$ and treat the DOW curve as its consequence, then constrain that curve two ways. The two real cases bracket it: Gaza (short hold, copes, CFR 6.7%) and Ukraine (long hold, prehospital death dominates). A sweep then runs the unobserved rate over a bounded range fixed by the TML+ curves, the FM 4-02 evacuation-policy days, and the observed Ukraine hours-to-days.
The open parameter: $f_{\text{surg}}$. The fraction of WIA needing forward surgery lives in the CUI estimator tools (MCET / MPTk), outside open doctrine. Converging open anchors pin it: ≈$0.10$–$0.12$ as a fraction of all WIA (the FST "10–15% of WIA need surgery" planning factor; Soroka's measured ~12% of arrivals), rising to ≈$0.20$–$0.34$ as the operative fraction of casualties who actually present to the node (Staudt, n≈11,000). Which value applies depends on the denominator, and the daily-average load moves with it, but the saturation finding rides on the within-pulse burst, so it survives across the bracket.

Parameters: pinned from outside the model, or tested inside it

The capacity numbers and the casualty rates are pinned by published doctrine and observed data. Only the modeling choices above are ours, and we report across them.

Fixed by published doctrine

ParameterDoctrinal sourceValue / role
Surgical service capacity ($s$, $\mu$)ATP 4-02.25 FRSD, Table 4-1 + para 2-3 ATP 4-02.252 operating tables; 135 min/case; sustained cap 10 cases/24 h / 30 cases/72 h before rest-and-resupply. This is $s\mu$.
Patient-holding windowATP 4-02.5 Casualty Care ATP 4-02.5The FRSD/Role 2 holds a patient up to 72 h before onward evacuation, capping queue residence at the node. Distinct from the equipment-set endurance (30 cases/72 h) and the Role 1 prolonged-field-care hold.
Theater evacuation policy (days held before onward evac)FM 4-02 §8-5 (7-day worked example); JP 4-02 FM 4-02The lever. SecDef-set, in days; sets time-in-system, which maps to the abandonment rate $\theta$.
Echelon structure (Role 1→4), triage categoriesFM 4-02; JP 4-02Queue topology and priority discipline.

Estimated from observed data

ParameterValue (source)Quality
Service time $1/\mu$ by injury severity94–206 min mean by ISS band, heavy right tail (Hall 2023, DoD Trauma Registry) Hall'23Best. Definitive-care time, an upper bound on forward damage-control.
Casualty arrival rate $\lambda$Doctrinal low-intensity anchor: ATP 4-02.55 Table D-4h (Korea, lower-intensity offensive), WIA ≈ 3.54/1,000/day ATP 4-02.55. Field calibration: battalion ~9.5%/day, division ~1%/day (Jones) Jones'19Good: doctrine plus official Army statistics.
Arrival burstiness83 casualties/hr peak, 673/24 h at one node (Oct-7 2023) Bahouth'24; heavy-tailed/multifractal (Lauren) Lauren'03Good: a measured pulse, so $\lambda(t)$ is time-varying.
Survival/abandonment clock $\theta(t)$ by severityTML+ dynamic mortality curves (NHRC 04-31): probability of death vs time-to-treatment TML+. Golden-hour evidence (Kotwal: surgery <1 h cut mortality 40%)Moderate→good: a doctrinal hazard.
DOW baseline (validation target)Post-9/11: 12.7% of deaths after reaching an MTF, 87.3% prehospital (Eastridge) Eastridge'12. Battlefield survivability 90% (vs 84% Vietnam, 80% WWII).Best baseline; the LSCO target is the gap (see limitation).
Evac delay (the lever, observed)Contested (Ukraine): hours–days; 24 h at POI, 48 h on the battlefield Lawry'25. Uncontested (Gaza): <70 min, CFR 6.7% Katzman'25Good: the two boundary cases bracket the curve.
Why the threshold is not tautological. $\lambda^{*}=s\mu$ moves only if a doctrinal number or an observed number changes; no analyst preference sits on that path. The DOW curve above the threshold is a theorem of the overload limit. Two prior studies lacked this property: Apache, whose driving parameter was a free analyst knob, and the blockade question, where the measurable proxy never pinned the estimand.

Work plan

PhaseActionStatus
0Question scoping, prior-art gate, literature sweep and PDF librarydone
1Calibration table: doctrinal $s,\mu,T$ from ATP/FM 4-02; $\lambda$, service time, survival clock, DOW from the empirical set: each cell with a source and a quality flagdone
2Test H1: compute $s\mu$ for a doctrinal node, compare to $\lambda_{\text{obs}}$: the go/no-go for the thesisdone
3Derive $\lambda^{*}$, the DOW elbow, and $\partial\,\text{DOW}/\partial T$ from the committed engine (estimand.js); the $M/G/c+G$ simulation is the working core, Erlang-A the closed-form skeletondone
4Validate against the Gaza/Ukraine brackets (direction reproduced); Tier-C robustness via the 40k-row sweepdone
5Write and submit (Military Operations Research)drafting

3 · The model

The forward surgical team is a waiting line in which the wounded can die before they reach a table. A capacity tally that asks only whether the team is over or under its daily load misses those deaths, because they happen inside the wait.

The model computes one number a planner can act on: the casualty rate above which the team can no longer keep up and patients begin dying in the queue, and how far each lever moves that rate. The threshold is a closed-form result from forty years of queueing theory, and a discrete-event simulation of the same node reproduces it. The interactive version lives in the Sim tab.

The abandonment queue and its overload limit

The forward surgical node, the Forward Resuscitative Surgical Detachment (FRSD), is a finite-capacity, many-server queue with abandonment. Wounded arrive at rate $\lambda$; the FRSD's two tables serve at a rate doctrine fixes (135 min/case, capped at 10 cases/24 h)ATP 4-02.25; a casualty who is not in service before the survival window closes leaves the queue by dying. That is the died-of-wounds mechanism, modeled as reneging, with hazard $\theta(t)$ from the TML+ mortality curvesTML+.

In the tractable reduction the node is the Erlang-A queue, $M/M/s+M$: Poisson arrivals, $s$ servers, exponential service rate $\mu$, and an exponential patience rate $\theta$ that stands in for the survival clockGarnett'02. Capacity is $s\mu$ and utilization is $\rho=\lambda/(s\mu)$. The abandonment fraction, our DOW, stays well defined as $\rho\to 1$ and beyond.

The threshold (Whitt, efficiency-driven regime)Whitt'04Whitt'06: $$\lambda^{*}=s\mu, \qquad \text{DOW}(\lambda)\;\approx\;\left(1-\frac{s\mu}{\lambda}\right)^{\!+}.$$ Below $\lambda^{*}$ the node keeps up and DOW $\to 0$. Above it, served throughput caps at $s\mu$ and the excess inflow $\lambda-s\mu$ dies waiting. So $\lambda^{*}$ is a sharp elbow in DOW-versus-$\lambda$, and to first order the curve is distribution-free: the shape of casualty arrivals and patience enters only through the realized wait. That holds even though real casualties and survival times are not exponential.

What the queue adds over a capacity tally. A capacity-versus-demand tally returns over or under. The queue returns four quantities that bear on the resourcing call:

  1. The shape. DOW rises as $\left(1-s\mu/\lambda\right)^{+}$, an elbow. The curve gives how many more die per increment of overload, so it tells a force designer whether 10% more capacity recovers 5% of the deaths or 30%.
  2. A bounded answer. A queue without abandonment either stays stable or grows an unbounded line. Because casualties leave the queue by dying, the death fraction stays finite and computable even when inflow exceeds capacityWhitt'06.
  3. The burst. A node under capacity on the daily average still saturates inside each pulse and recovers between themBahouth'24. The daily average misses those deaths; only time-varying arrivals capture themEick'93.
  4. The lever. Contested transport leaves both $\mu$ and $\lambda$ untouched and lengthens holding time, which never appears in demand-versus-capacity. The queue turns that delay into deaths per added day.

How evacuation policy enters. Theater evacuation policy sets the longest a patient is held before onward movement. A threatened forward leg or a saturated strategic pipeline lengthens that hold; a longer hold keeps casualties in the system and lowers the effective abandonment rate $\theta$, since more of the survival window is spent waiting. The Erlang-A elasticitiesWhitt'06b turn that into the days of holding the system absorbs before DOW steps up.

What the model returns.

  1. The binding constraint is operating-table-hours. Capacity is $s\mu$, set by surgery, so the threshold sits well below the bed-count intuition the force reports against (Hospital Center ≤240 beds, down from >13,000 in 1991)AHEC.
  2. A quantified golden-hour collapse: $\lambda^{*}$ is a specific casualty rate, set against observed LSCO rates.
  3. The evacuation-policy sensitivity: deaths per added holding-day, a number a planner can act on.

Refinements layer back on the realism the closed form abstracts away: $M/G/c+G$ service and patience (Hall service times, a severity-indexed survival clock); time-varying arrivals by modified-offered-loadGreen'07 for the pulses; severity classes as a priority/clearing modelArgon'08Mills'13. The MASCAL sibling (Q232) is the batch-arrival variant.

▶ Run it yourself. The sandbox in the Sim tab exposes the model as controls: switch service from $M$ to $G$, turn the survival clock on or off, cap the waiting room, and pulse the arrivals. Press Sweep the load to draw the elbow live and watch the overload law $(1-c\mu/\lambda)$ emerge. The numbers come from a discrete-event engine run to 40,000 cases.

What's been tried, and the gap

Each existing approach answers part of the question and stops short of a threshold with a mechanism behind it. Capacity-versus-demand arithmetic gives a binary. Discrete-event casualty simulations (SIMEDIS / NATO Vigorous Warrior; RAND JMPT; NPS FST sims SIMEDISRAND'21) give one scenario's number at a time, which is why this study uses simulation to validate the law and the closed form to state it. Capacity optimization allocates resources without characterizing the cliffBouma'05. MEDEVAC dispatch MDPs put the server on the aircraft, where the bottleneck is transportJenkins'18. System-dynamics flow models average out the reneging elbow. The one analytic surgical queue is civilian and treats overflow as patients turned away at the door, with no doctrinal $\mu$Ferreira'24. Qualitative LSCO casualty-care work says the golden hour breaks but names no rateRemondelli'23.

No published model derives an analytic saturation threshold for the forward surgical node with the service rate fixed by published doctrine and died-of-wounds modeled as queue abandonment. Three search passes, including an NPS Calhoun REST-API sweep, found the queueing machinery, the simulations, and one civilian loss-model. The full source list is in References; four annotated PDF sets sit under papers/.
Why it is still open. The problem sits between two communities that rarely talk. Army medical planners answer it with simulations that give one scenario's number and neither generalize nor explain. Queueing theorists built the abandonment apparatus for call centers, where reneging means a customer hung up. Identifying died-of-wounds with reneging connects the two, and two decades of theory then bear on a question the defense literature has only simulated. The contribution is that connection and the calibration to doctrine and observed rates on top of it. Healthcare queueing is otherwise a settled application areaGreen'06.

4 · Results

The casualty surge overruns the forward surgical team while the daily average stays inside its margin. The strongest lever on who lives is how fast the wounded reach the table. Transport limits outcomes in two separate ways: the wounded reach surgery only as fast as the threat allows, and they leave theater only as fast as the lift allows. Each limit takes its own fix.

Two findings rest on measured data: the surge and the evacuation timeline. The rest follow from how any finite-server queue with a survival clock behaves, so they frame the decision and do not stand as evidence about this war; the headers mark which is which. Two independent re-runs of the engines reproduced every number below.

Findings grounded in data

1 · The surge saturates the team below capacity data-forced

Doctrine sizes the forward surgical node to an average casualty rate: ATP 4-02.55's lower-intensity offensive rate (Korea), WIA ≈ 3.54 per 1,000 per dayATP 4-02.55, against a node rated for ~10 cases in 24 hATP 4-02.25. On that arithmetic the node copes. The arithmetic is wrong, because casualties arrive in pulses, and the pulse is measured: 83 casualties in one hour at a single facility on 7 OctoberSoroka'24, against two tables clearing about one surgical case an hour. Hold the daily load fixed and change only the arrival pattern:

Rate regime$\lambda$ /day$\rho$steady DOWbatch $\sigma{=}1.0$batch $\sigma{=}1.4$
Korea, lower-intensity offensive15.90.211.6%23.1%36.5%
WWII-Europe offensive43.80.5910.4%31.1%42.3%
WWII-Italy offensive70.20.9422.0%38.3%47.4%

$\rho$ is load against surge table-capacity ($c\mu\approx$ 21 cases/day). Each row holds $\rho$ fixed and changes only the arrival pattern, so the steady-versus-pulse gap stands whatever the daily-average level turns out to be.

At the Korea rate the node runs at 21% of capacity, yet pulsed arrivals kill 23% against 1.6% for the same daily load arriving steadily. A mass-casualty event delivers a day of surgery in an afternoon, and at 135 minutes a case two tables cannot keep up; the deaths come from arrivals exceeding throughput during the pulse. The pulse and the throughput are both measured numbers, while the death fraction's level tracks the survival clock, which we sweep. So what: the doctrinal rule sizes capacity against the mean rate, and the binding planning quantity is the surge.

2 · Evacuation time sets the surgical death rate data-grounded

The evidence is a natural experiment: two wars whose wounds were comparable but whose evacuation times diverged, so the gap in outcomes reads straight off that one difference. With evacuation under 70 minutes and whole blood forward, Gaza held case-fatality at 6.7%Katzman'25; with a contested forward zone forcing holds of hours to days, Ukrainian casualties die before a surgeon sees themLawry'25. Eastridge fixes the scale of that window: 87% of combat deaths occur before the hospitalEastridge'12, and the golden-hour evidence makes it causal, with evacuation inside the hour cutting mortality sharplyKotwal. The model carries this forward: as the rotary air-evacuation fraction falls (the forward MEDEVAC lane, distinct from theater air control), total mortality rises and the dying shifts off the table and onto the road.

Rotary air-evac fractionprehospitaltotal mortalitywhere death falls (pre : at-node)
95% (sky owned)18.8%42.0%45 / 55
60%40.2%52.9%76 / 24
20% (contested)64.4%68.8%94 / 6

The model scores access (death in transit or in the queue) and credits a save at table contact, leaving clinical survival on the table to the trauma literature. The where death falls split, prehospital versus at-the-node as shares of total mortality, is the comparison that holds across runs; the finding is the shift in that split.

The forward shift is monotone across every parameter we vary, and its level holds given the hours-to-days ground evacuation a contested forward zone imposes. This is the first of two transport bottlenecks, the threat-limited one: the leg from point of injury to the surgical node, degraded by contested air. More helicopters cannot resource around it, because rotary lift is what the enemy shoots first, so the levers that still work are the ones that need no air or ground route (prolonged field care, blood pushed forward; Finding 3). So what: evacuation time is a surgical-outcome variable, so air superiority over the forward fight belongs inside the medical estimate, and the casualty forecast turns on more than wound severity.

The threshold and the curve, computed

Three quantities pin Findings 1 and 2 to exact numbers, all computed from the committed engine (calibration/estimand.js): the saturation threshold $\lambda^{*}$, the overload curve $\text{DOW}(\lambda)$, and the evacuation-timeline sensitivity $\partial\,\text{DOW}/\partial T$. The first two fix where Finding 1's saturation begins; the slope turns Finding 2's natural experiment into a function of delay.

The threshold $\lambda^{*}=s\mu$

For the FRSD (two tables, 135 min a case) sustained surgical throughput is $s\mu = 2\times(1440/135) \approx 21$ operative cases per day at continuous run. In all-WIA terms, at the presenter operative fraction (0.286), the node crosses $\rho=1$ near 75 WIA per day, just above the WWII-Italy-offensive rate (15.6 per 1,000 × a 4,500-soldier catchment, $\rho\approx0.94$). The doctrinal node sits just below the threshold at the most intense historical offensive rate: wide margin at the Korea rate ($\rho\approx0.21$), all but critically loaded at the hardest offensive rate.

The elbow $\text{DOW}(\lambda)$ H2

Sweeping the load through the threshold gives the curve, which tracks the fluid overload law $\left(1-s\mu/\lambda\right)^{+}$ above $\rho=1$:

all-WIA $\lambda$ /day$\rho$DOWfluid law $(1-s\mu/\lambda)^{+}$
16 (Korea)0.211.6%0
44 (Europe off.)0.5910%0
70 (Italy off.)0.9422%0
751.0024%0
901.2130%17%
1602.1455%53%

The threshold holds at $\lambda^{*}=s\mu$, and the fluid law is the above-threshold asymptote the simulation converges to by $\rho\approx2$. The transition is a soft elbow: a short survival clock makes abandonment nonzero below threshold, with casualties reneging while waiting even at $\rho<1$, so saturation begins before $\rho=1$. That below-threshold reneging is itself a finding (a small-server queue against a short clock loses double digits at moderate load), and it leaves H2 right in location but gentler in shape than $\left(1-s\mu/\lambda\right)^{+}$ alone.

The evacuation-timeline slope $\partial\,\text{DOW}/\partial T$ H4

Holding the hard LSCO load fixed and varying only $T$, the time from wounding to surgery, gives a positive, steep slope, steepest in the first hour: the model produces the golden hour rather than assuming it.

$T$ to surgerytotal mortalitywhere it falls (pre : node)marginal $\partial\,\text{DOW}/\partial T$
45 min40%39 / 61–
60 min  (Gaza)44%54 / 46~16 pt / h
120 min59%85 / 15~14 pt / h
240 min78%98 / 2~8 pt / h
720 min  (Ukraine)97%100 / 0~1 pt / h

The first added hour of delay costs ~16 mortality points, the eighth almost nothing, because by then everyone who will die in transit has. The golden hour is the interval where $\partial\,\text{DOW}/\partial T$ is steepest. Validation: the Gaza (fast to surgery) and Ukraine (hours-to-days) endpoints bracket the curve in the direction the natural experiment demands, fast copes and slow does not, falsifying the null $\partial\,\text{DOW}/\partial T\approx0$. The absolute levels ride the swept survival clock; the steep monotone rise and the bracket ordering are what we claim.

This slope is the air-superiority finding (Finding 2) in another coordinate: rotary-evacuation viability sets $T$, so the timeline sensitivity and the headline data finding are one mechanism ($T=45$ min reproduces the sky-owned 40% floor of the envelope). And the timeline here is the forward one, time-to-surgery, which the Gaza/Ukraine bracket tests. The doctrinal rear "days held before onward evacuation" is a separate effect, the back-pressure of Finding 4 below, which the model exposes as structure rather than confirms as a tested hypothesis.

Structure the model exposes

These follow from the two findings above and the shape of any finite-server queue with a survival clock. They organize the resourcing decision; treat them as structure.

3 · The best lever depends on the air picture

As the forward air picture changes, death moves between the node and the road, and the lever that helps moves with it. With the sky owned, the node is the bottleneck and surgical capacity buys the most; as air is lost, the levers that buy the casualty time take over, led by prolonged field care.

Intervention (Δ total mortality)air 20%air 60%air 90%
Prolonged field care (survival span ×2)−18.6−14.8−13.0
More tables (c: 2→4)−3.4−9.2−14.2
Faster surgery ($\mu$: 135→90)−1.3−3.6−5.7

The logic follows from the first two findings; the crossover location is the model's output. So what: the procurement answer, tables or time, is conditional on whether the force will own the air, so the medical force design depends on stating that assumption.

One caution the table hides: prolonged field care enters the model as a dial, double the survival span and read off the −18.6 points. The field charges for that dial. Holding a casualty 72 hours forward takes trained teams, sustained labor, and Class VIII (blood, oxygen, analgesia, consumables) at the casualty collection point and Role 1, echelons that today carry neither the supplies nor the training to do it; the Role 2 has a limited patient-hold capability. The model says forward holding is the highest-value lever as air is lost, and it leaves open which of training, manning, Class VIII, and policy is the binding shortfall. That shortfall is what the −18.6 points actually cost.

Forward whole blood sits just outside this scope: pushing resuscitation toward the point of injury would buy survival time on the body even where surgical teams cannot be distributed. Its precise effect on the survival clock is not pinned, so the model leaves it as a future extension rather than scoring it here.

4 · A throughput-limited strategic evacuation can stall the forward node (the second axis)

The forward node's death rate holds flat until strategic lift crosses ~92% utilization, then it jumps. The rear bottleneck is the lift: Madigan, Walter Reed, and BAMC flex and offload to civilian trauma systems, so definitive-care beds have room. What binds is moving litter patients out of theater by strategic aeromedical evacuation, a fleet of litter-configured cargo aircraft and the crews that provide care en route, throughput-limited and flown from rear airfields that near-peer long-range fires could still reach (a scope boundary we return to). When it cannot clear the theater fast enough, stabilized patients cannot leave the forward node's holding, a finished surgical case cannot vacate the table, and the tables block while surgeons idle. This is the generic behavior of a tandem queue whose downstream stage saturates, and it appears as a cliff.

Strategic-evac liftprehospitalforward nodetotal mortalitynode Δ from blocking
Ample (clears the theater)40.1%21.4%52.9%–
Saturated (theater cut off)40.1%26.9%56.2%+5.5 pt

Adding one strategic-evac slot cuts node death about five points; doubling the forward holding (8→16 beds) cuts it about two. The back-pressure increment is forward-node death the rear pipeline causes, a third place to die on the second axis, and the wounded back up faster than buffer can absorb, so the fix is airframes. The squeeze eases as forward air is lost, because fewer wounded survive transit to need a flight out, so the two transport axes are substitutes: the rear pipeline binds hardest exactly when the forward fight goes well.

So what: when forward evacuation succeeds, the volume of survivors overwhelms an under-resourced strategic evacuation, so the rear-evacuation fleet is part of the Army surgical node's effective capacity. This is a procurement problem, and it spans airframes plus the crewed en-route care (critical-care air transport teams) and the patient-regulation pipeline, which in a real theater bind as often as the aircraft count. The capacity numbers and the cliff's exact location track the buffer size and stay illustrative; what holds is the mechanism, and that the relief is downstream lift.

5 · Triage precedence is a second-order lever, and the obvious rule backfires

Triage at both choke points (who gets the next free table, who gets the fast rotary lane) moves total operative mortality only 1 to 2 points, and on the air slot the intuitive rule runs the wrong way. A precedence engine (calibration/triage_test.js, validated to reproduce the baseline air sweep) sets the precedence and measures what it buys, against a baseline that serves first-in-first-out and routes the air slot at random.

Rotary air-evactable: earliest-deadline vs FIFOair: most-urgent firstair: salvageable first
95% (sky owned)−1.4+0.3+0.1
60%−1.0+0.7−1.7
20% (contested)−0.4+1.0−1.4

Δ total operative mortality, points; negative helps. Table precedence, serving the casualty nearest his deadline, buys up to about 1.5 points, most when the sky is owned and the node queue is the long one. Air precedence carries a trap: routing the fast slot to the most urgent casualty beats random at no air level, because the shortest-clock casualties are disproportionately the ones a fast evacuation cannot save, so scarce lift goes to the dying while the salvageable take the road. Routing it to the salvageable instead, those a fast evac saves and a slow one loses, helps ~1.5 points, most in the middle of the air range where slots are scarce but not vanishing.

So what: triage of scarce evacuation is worth setting at a combined best near 2 to 2.5 points, well below prolonged field care or forward blood, and the doctrinally intuitive "sickest first" routing of aircraft is counterproductive. The rule ordering and the directions are the finding; the levels ride the swept survival clock. This is the impatient-jobs clearing model the Methods tab formalizes, now run in a committed simulation.

Tested, but not yet a claim

Hardening a single evacuation route

Speeding one ground corridor and then losing it to interdiction can leave the force worse off than the slow road it started with. The direction holds, but it rests on an adversary-response multiplier we impose rather than measure, so take it as a caution and not a result: concentrating flow on an interdictable asset is fragile. Route redundancy is the doctrinal hedge.

What sets the levels

The orderings above hold across the sweep; four inputs set their scale, and we report each as swept rather than pinned to a single value:

  • The survival-from-wounding clock is the largest unknown, since no measured untreated-survival curve exists for the operative population, and the death levels move most with it. The directions hold as it varies.
  • Whether the daily-average load exceeds capacity depends on the fraction of wounded who present to the node, which open doctrine does not give. The burst and evacuation findings hold regardless, because neither rests on that fraction.
  • The model scores who reaches surgery in time, so it speaks to access (death in transit or in line) and leaves clinical survival, the patient who reaches the table and dies anyway, to the trauma literature.
  • Operative need, the survival clock, and surgery duration are drawn independently, where in reality the worst-wounded are at once the most likely to need surgery, the quickest to die, and the longest on the table. Coupling them raises the death fraction, so the reported levels stay conservative and the orderings do not depend on it.

The envelope: best day, worst day

The findings above each move within a range the dataset measures, and read as corners they bound the outcome: a good day, when the sky is owned and casualties trickle in, against a bad day, when air is contested, the wounds are fast, and the pulse lands all at once. Each row holds everything else fixed and swings one parameter across its committed grid, so the rows have different baselines and each reports the swing in the right column (24 seeds per cell, max SEM 0.16 pt).

Parameter (axis)best-day cornerworst-day cornermortality swing
Air superiority, forward (E5)sky owned: 40.3%contested: 78.5%+38 pt
Arrival pattern at fixed load, forward (E1)steady: 1.6%pulsed $\sigma{=}1.4$: 36.5%+35 pt
Surgery duration $\mu$, forward (E3)135 min: 31.1%200 min: 41.0%+10 pt
Survival clock, sets both axes (E6)long span: 38.1%short span: 72.1%+34 pt
Strategic-evac lift, rear (E8)ample: node 28.4%cut off: node 42.1%+14 pt*

*The rear corner is a node-death increment with the sky owned, on a different axis and denominator from the forward corners, and (see below) it stays off the forward total. Arrival pattern (E1) holds load fixed and varies only the pulse, so its swing is denominator-invariant; the air, $\mu$, clock, and lift swings ride the survival clock, which we sweep, so the directions are anchored and the levels swept.

The operative-mortality envelope runs from roughly 40% on a good day to the high 70s on a bad one. The good day is the air corner with the sky owned (40%), already a sobering floor: even with golden-hour evacuation, the LSCO wound mix and the pulse kill two in five who reach surgical consideration. The bad day is contested air (79%), where most of the dying has moved off the table and onto the road. The clock and the burst slide the whole band up or down inside that range.

One stacking rule the data enforces: do not add the worst forward corner to the worst rear corner. The two transport axes are substitutes. The rear back-pressure penalty is +14 points when the sky is owned and falls to +0.2 points when air is contested, because losing forward air kills the wounded in transit, so fewer survive to saturate strategic evacuation. The rear pipeline binds hardest on the good forward day, and on the bad forward day it is already empty. The worst single number the model produces is therefore the contested-air forward corner alone, with the rear penalty correctly near zero; adding the cut-rear corner on top would double-count. The envelope is a band you slide along two axes.

What this means for force design

Size the forward node for the pulse: the binding number is the surge, and the mean-rate planning factor understates it by an order of magnitude. Then treat transport as two distinct bottlenecks, because they fail for opposite reasons and take opposite fixes. The forward leg, point of injury to the table, is threat-limited: contested air kills the wounded in transit, and aircraft the enemy destroys first cannot buy a way out, so the levers that still work are the ones that need no air or ground route (blood and prolonged field care pushed forward), and their real cost is training and Class VIII at echelons that lack both. The rear leg, node to definitive care out of theater, is capacity-limited: the CONUS hospitals flex, but the Air Force aeromedical-evacuation fleet that feeds them is too small, and it binds hardest when forward evacuation is succeeding. That leg is a procurement problem solved with airframes. The medical force design turns on naming which fight, forward or rear, the force expects to come up short in.

5 · Discussion, Limitations & So-What

The takeaway for leaders

Bottom line
The forward surgical node is sized to the peer planning casualty rate. The intense phase of a great-power war overruns it inside the engagement pulse, and past that point the queue kills a share of the dead the wound would have spared.

That preventable share is the only thing resourcing can buy back, and the bed counts the force reports never show it. Five calls fall out of that:

  • Grow throughput. Operating-table-hours bind first, set by surgical teams. The capacity target is throughput that meets the engagement-pulse arrival; the daily average understates it, and a bed count measures the wrong thing.
  • The decision number is deaths per added holding-day, $\partial\,\text{DOW}/\partial T$, a slope. It says exactly what a longer evacuation policy costs in lives.
  • Transport is two bottlenecks that take opposite fixes. The forward leg, point of injury to the table, is threat-limited: contested air kills the wounded in transit, and aircraft the enemy destroys first cannot buy you out of it. The rear leg, node to definitive care out of theater, is capacity-limited: the CONUS hospitals flex, but the Air Force strategic-evacuation fleet that feeds them is too small. Forward you change tactics and push capability onto the body; rear you buy airframes. Naming which one the force expects to come up short in is the design choice.
  • Prolonged field care is the highest-value forward lever, and it is a bill. As air is lost, buying the casualty time outranks a surgical table he cannot reach in time. Holding a casualty forward for days takes trained teams, manning, and Class VIII at the collection point and Role 1, which carry neither today; the Role 2 hold is limited. The model prices the value, and the decision is which of training, manning, supply, and policy is the binding shortfall.
  • Force structure is a bet on the casualty-rate regime. Build to the planning rate and the assault phase produces preventable death; build to the offensive rate and most of the capacity sits idle in sustainment. The model prices both sides of that bet.

The tipping point: what holds, what moves it

The tipping point's location is fixed and checkable ($\lambda^{*}=s\mu$, doctrine-pinned, the go/no-go inequality). The unobservable inputs move its steepness, not where it sits. Each perturbation below acts on one of those two, and none of them moves the threshold.

Perturbation (Δ)Effect on the tipping point
A: service-time distribution shape (variance, tails)Location $\lambda^{*}=s\mu$ holds; mild effect on steepness. (The Whitt insensitivity result: the sim shows $M/D/G$ nearly coincide above the elbow.)
B: survival-time (patience) distribution shapeLocation holds; moves steepness and level.
C: unobservable contested-rear baseline DOW rateLocation holds (it is capacity versus arrivals); sets the level of the curve above the elbow. The one number we cannot see touches only the part that is already a sensitivity sweep.
¬X: the strategic pipeline binds before tablesShifts the binding constraint downstream. When strategic-evac lift saturates, clearing capacity out of theater sets the threshold, and the lever shifts to airframes (the second axis).
¬Y: most DOW is wound-fatal regardless of timingShrinks the prize. The controllable (capacity-limited) slice is small; the model still locates the elbow, but the decision payoff falls.
¬Z dynamic: μ falls under load (fatigue, blood-out)Bends the frame. Capacity is endogenous, $\lambda^{*}$ drifts left as the battle wears on, and resupply and rotation become a first-order lever.

Sensitivity across the two transport axes

Transport fails for two opposite reasons, and the medical estimate has to size against the right one. Lump them as a single "contested rear" and both the mechanism and the fix disappear.

Axis 1: the forward fight gets more contested (threat-limited). The contested zone is forward: the leg from point of injury to the node, and the node's own supply lines. A worsening forward fight fans into three paths, and all three push death the same way.

PathTraceEffect
1: tactical evac degradedcontested air → casualty evacuation (POI→node) slows → survival clock runs out in transit → prehospital death.Raises total mortality and shifts the dying forward, off the table and onto the road (the air-superiority sweep). Bought back only by levers that ride no contested lane.
2: forward resupply interdictedblood (VIIIB) and materiel (VIIIA) to the node interdicted → damage-control resuscitation is blood-limited (ATP 4-02.25 ¶1-11)ATP 4-02.25 → $\mu$ ↓.Moves $\lambda^{*}$ left. Holding longer does not touch it; the same fight that slows evac starves the blood that holding requires.
3: forced displacementnode disperses/displaces to survive → produces zero while moving (ATP 4-02.25 Ch 4) → time-averaged $\mu$ ↓.Moves $\lambda^{*}$ left and makes $\mu$ time-varying: capacity decays as the battle wears on (the ¬Z-dynamic regime).

These three compound, worsening together as the forward fight does, and more aircraft buy back none of them, because the aircraft are what the enemy destroys first. What works puts the capability where the enemy cannot reach it: prolonged field care and blood on the body, dispersion to lower each node's exposure.

Axis 2: the strategic pipeline saturates (capacity-limited). Out of theater the definitive-care hospitals flex, and the aeromedical-evacuation flights run from rear airfields. What binds is the strategic evacuation fleet: too few litter-configured aircraft, and as often too few critical-care air-transport crews and too little patient-regulation throughput to move the stabilized outflow. When strategic lift cannot clear the theater, holding fills and the forward tables block from behind, the back-pressure cliff. Capacity drives this axis, and it bites hardest when forward evacuation is succeeding and survivors are plentiful. Procurement relieves it, by adding airframes and crews; tactics do not.

Scope boundary. We model the rear as capacity-limited. A near-peer whose long-range fires range the aerial ports of embarkation and the en-route hubs turns part of the rear into a threat problem too, and the two axes collapse back toward each other. That is the case where the split here breaks, and it is a different model; we hold it aside as a separate study. The capacity-limited rear is the base case.
Read-off. The two axes are substitutes: they do not add their worst cases. Lose the forward air and casualties die in transit, so fewer survive to need a flight out; the rear pipeline binds hardest exactly when the forward fight goes well. The contested-forward case and the saturated-strategic-evac case are different fights with different fixes. Forward is threat-limited and answered by capability pushed onto the body (PFC, blood) and dispersion; rear is capacity-limited and answered by procurement (airframes, litter kits, port throughput). The medical estimate has to name which one it is sizing against.

Three meanings of "contested," kept distinct. The unobservable clinical DOW baseline (robustness row C) moves only the steepness of the curve, leaving the location safe. The forward logistics contestation traced here moves the location, through $\mu$. The strategic-evac shortfall is a downstream capacity that blocks the front from behind. Three different objects, one overloaded phrase.

Where the closed form runs out. Back-pressure (tables blocked by a saturated strategic pipeline), a capacity $\mu(t)$ that decays as the forward fight wears on, and bursty arrivals together have no closed form. The Erlang-A elbow is the static special case; once these enter, the working model is a finite-capacity abandonment-queue simulation.

Limitations

Three limits bound the result, with the full statement in the Plan. The DOW magnitude is a derived output that no published contested-rear clinical DOW rate validates, so we report it as a model output; the threshold $\lambda^{*}=s\mu$ does not depend on that unknown, and the headline holds regardless. The surgical fraction $f_{\text{surg}}$ is CUI, pinned from converging open anchors (≈0.10–0.12 of WIA, ≈0.20–0.34 of presenters), and the within-pulse saturation finding holds across that range. The second axis enters only as a throughput constraint on the forward node: strategic aeromedical evacuation is a downstream lift capacity that blocks or clears, modeled short of a fleet to be scheduled. Sizing and routing that fleet (how many airframes, on what cycle, to hold theater clearing under a given casualty stream) is the adjacent problem this work points to.

Where the result stands

  • The decision is the same one throughout: minimize the preventable (capacity-limited) DOW slice, across the levers of capacity, positioning, evac policy, evac throughput, triage, and resupply.
  • Three assumptions carry it, each one named and falsifiable: X, tables bind; Y, DOW is waiting-dominated; Z, service is work-conserving and finite-mean.
  • The threshold survives the unknowns. Perturbations A, B, and C move the steepness of the curve; none moves its location at $\lambda^{*}=s\mu$.
  • The go/no-go inequality is now decidable and the estimand is in hand: $\lambda^{*}=s\mu$ (the FRSD crosses $\rho=1$ near the Italy-offensive rate), the DOW elbow (location at $\lambda^{*}$, softened below threshold by the survival clock), and the evacuation-timeline slope $\partial\,\text{DOW}/\partial T$ (~16 mortality points per added hour in the first hour, the golden hour emerging from the model), with the Gaza/Ukraine bracket reproducing its direction. Left to do: the $M/G/c+G$ refinements and a triage-precedence engine, on top of the core derivation already standing.

6 · References

Companion BibTeX: references.bib (same folder). Every entry has a URL to verify the document or doctrine; citation markers in the other tabs jump here. VERIFIED = downloaded this session · PAYWALL/CONTROLLED = access blocked, retrieve via browser/CAC. PDFs under papers/0X_*/.

§A · Doctrine & casualty-estimation

ATP 4-02.25, The FRSD (2020): fixes $\mu$ (Table 4-1: 2 tables, 135 min/case, 10 cases/24 h, 30/72 h). atp4-02-25_frsd_2020.pdf
armypubs.army.mil/…/ARN37250-ATP_4-02.25
ATP 4-02.5, Casualty Care (2013): Role-2 72-h holding window. atp4-02-5_casualty-care_2013.pdf
armypubs.army.mil/…/ARN30121-ATP_4-02.5
ATP 4-02.55, AHS Support Planning (2020): fixes $\lambda$ (Table D-4a; Table D-4e Mideast peer ≈ 4.25/1,000/day; D-4c/d WWII offensive 9.74–15.6 WIA). atp4-02-55_ahs-planning_2020.pdf
irp.fas.org/doddir/army/atp4-02-55.pdf (opens in a browser) · Wayback mirror
FM 4-02, Army Health System (2013) §8-5: fixes the evac-policy lever (SecDef-set, in days; 7-day example). fm4-02_ahs_2013.pdf
globalsecurity.org/…/fm4-02-2013.pdf
Mitchell et al., TML+ Dynamic Casualty Mortality Curves (NHRC 04-31): parameterizes the reneging hazard $\theta(t)$. tmlplus_mortality-curves_ada433701.pdf
archive.org/…/DTIC_ADA433701.pdf
Lauren (2003), "On the Temporal Distribution of Casualties," NZ DTA Report 187: bursty, heavy-tailed arrivals. lauren_temporal-distribution-casualties_2003.pdf
arxiv.org/abs/1204.0050
Sheets et al., "Army Medical Capacity: Ready to Meet the LSCO Challenge?" USAWC/AHEC: the capacity squeeze. ahec_army-medical-capacity-lsco.pdf
ahec.armywarcollege.edu/…/Army_Medical_Capacity.pdf
Also on disk: JP 4-02 (2017), ATP 4-02.2 (2019, Wayback), Hobbs CAA-TP-84-4 (DTIC ADA149644), ATTP 4-02 (2011), Zouris NHRC 13-61 / 00-17, Knight CASEVAC SAMS. URLs in papers/01_/ANNOTATED_BIB.md.
CONTROLLED CREStT / Medical Planners' Toolkit and the TML+ tool are CUI/CAC-gated; ATP 4-02.55 App-D is the open substitute for the rate set. The surgical fraction $f_{\text{surg}}$ lives in MCET / MPTk here.

§B · Methods: the queueing backbone VERIFIED (16 PDFs)

Garnett, Mandelbaum & Reiman (2002), "Designing a Call Center with Impatient Customers," M&SOM 4(3): the Erlang-A ($M/M/n+M$) model class; DOW = reneging.
aviman.technion.ac.il/…/Garnett.pdf
Whitt (2004), "Efficiency-Driven Heavy-Traffic Approximations for Many-Server Queues with Abandonments," Mgmt Sci 50(10): the overload regime; source of $\lambda^{*}$ and the DOW curve.
columbia.edu/~ww2040/efficiencyCOR.pdf
Whitt (2006), "Fluid Models for Multiserver Queues with Abandonments," Oper. Res. 54(1): distribution-general overload limit ($M/GI/s/r+GI$).
columbia.edu/~ww2040/fluid2006.pdf
Whitt (2006), "Sensitivity of Performance in the Erlang-A Model…," Queueing Syst. 51: elasticities for the evac-policy ($\theta$) sensitivity.
columbia.edu/~ww2040/elasticity.pdf
Zhang (2012), "Fluid Models of Many-Server Queues with Abandonment," Queueing Syst. 72 (arXiv:0909.1671): rigorous $G/GI/n+GI$ fluid limit.
arxiv.org/abs/0909.1671
van Leeuwaarden, Mathijsen & Zwart (2019), "Economies-of-Scale in Many-Server Queueing Systems," SIAM Review 61(3): open QED/Halfin–Whitt tutorial.
arxiv.org/abs/1706.05397
Eick, Massey & Whitt (1993), "The Physics of the Mt/G/∞ Queue," Oper. Res. 41(4): time-varying offered load for battle pulses.
columbia.edu/~ww2040/physics.pdf
Green, Kolesar & Whitt (2007), "Coping with Time-Varying Demand…," POM 16(1): the MOL recipe for nonstationary arrivals.
columbia.edu/~ww2040/Green_Kolesar_Whitt.pdf
Argon, Ziya & Righter (2008), "Scheduling Impatient Jobs in a Clearing System… Patient Triage in MASCAL," PEIS 22(3): lifetimes = survival windows.
ziya.web.unc.edu/…/Argon-Ziya-Righter-2008.pdf
Mills, Argon & Ziya (2013), "Resource-Based Patient Prioritization in MCIs," M&SOM 15(3): fluid triage with time-decaying survival.
ziya.web.unc.edu/…/Mills-Argon-Ziya-2013.pdf
Green (2006), "Queueing Analysis in Health Care," in Patient Flow (Springer): the servers-vs-beds lineage.
business.columbia.edu/…/QueueingAnalysis.pdf
Also: Fomundam & Herrmann (2007) healthcare-queueing survey; Eick-Massey-Whitt sinusoidal; Virtamo loss notes; Sun-Argon-Ziya austere triage. PAYWALL Halfin & Whitt (1981), Zeltyn & Mandelbaum (2005), covered by open substitutes. Full list in papers/02_/ANNOTATED_BIB.md.

§C · Empirical calibration

Hall et al. (2023), "Surgical Capability Utilization Time for Military Casualties at Role 2 and Role 3," Military Medicine 188(11-12):e3368. Service times by ISS (the $\mu$). PAYWALL (open full text on Oxford; browser save)
doi.org/10.1093/milmed/usac414
Eastridge et al. (2012), "Death on the Battlefield (2001–2011)," J Trauma 73(6 Suppl 5): post-9/11 DOW baseline. VERIFIED
east.org/…/Eastridge 2012
Remondelli et al. (2023), "Casualty Care Implications of LSCO," J Trauma 95(2 Suppl 1): casualties/day vignette; golden hour. VERIFIED · the 47.3%/88.2% figures are not here (see JRTC)
pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10389308
Jones, MAJ David R. (2019), "Large-Scale Combat Operations, Casualties, and the All-Volunteer Force," School of Advanced Military Studies, US Army CGSC (DTIC AD1083499): echelon casualty rates (the $\lambda$ skeleton; battalion ~9.5%/day). VERIFIED · author confirmed from the title page: earlier drafts mis-cited this as "Walters"
apps.dtic.mil/sti/pdfs/AD1083499.pdf
Lawry et al. (2025), "POI to Role 2+ combat casualty care in Ukraine," TSACO 10:e001674. Contested-rear evac delay. VERIFIED
pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12198803
Katzman, Kalman, Almog & Fogel (2025), "Deployment of Physician Resources … Israel–Hamas War … Impact on the Case Fatality Rate," Military Medicine 190(9-10):226–230 (commentary): the uncontested boundary case. IDF CFR fell 14.8% (2006) → 9.2% (2014) → 6.7% (2023–24) despite ISS>15 rising 8%→16%→23%, via forward Low-Titer O Whole Blood at the point of injury, physician forward medical teams, and <70-min evacuation; vs WWII 19.1%, Iraq/Afghanistan 9.4%. VERIFIED (full text on disk; all figures confirmed)
doi.org/10.1093/milmed/usae553 · PubMed 39671529
Bahouth et al. (2024), "October 7th 2023 MASCAL in southern Israel" (Soroka): the measured arrival pulse. VERIFIED
pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11556145
PAYWALL April et al. (2021), "Notional Casualties at the JRTC," Med J (AMEDD), PMID 34251658: true source of the 47.3% DOW figure (notional exercise output). Austin to retrieve.
pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34251658

§D · Adjacent models: prior art to distinguish

Jenkins, Robbins & Lunday (2018), MEDEVAC dispatch via MDP: closest neighbor; dispatch not treatment, no $\lambda^{*}$, no reneging. VERIFIED
apps.dtic.mil/sti/citations/AD1051615
Bouma (2005), METCOM: treatment-capacity optimization, not a saturation queue. VERIFIED
apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA439323.pdf
Ferreira (2024), "Studying a Surgery Service Occupation through a Queues Model": civilian, loss model. VERIFIED
arxiv.org/abs/2403.05554
Thomas et al. (2021), "Preparing for the Future of Combat Casualty Care," RAND RR-A713-1: "hold more, hold longer"; JMPT simulation. VERIFIED
rand.org/…/RAND_RRA713-1.pdf
"RL to optimize resource utilization in combat casualty care": optimization, not analytic saturation. VERIFIED
ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC12738704
NPS theses: Syvertson (FST simulation), Cobb (bed optimization): sim / optimization. VERIFIED
calhoun.nps.edu/handle/10945/31496
PAYWALL SIMEDIS / NATO Vigorous Warrior 2024, Military Medicine 189(Suppl 3):456. Canonical LSCO casualty-flow simulation. Austin to retrieve.
academic.oup.com/milmed/…/456
